classification.title: An Act to provide for the disposition of property of the Commonwealth of Kentucky at the Louisiana Purchase Exposition.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the commission for the Louisiana Purchase Exposition to sell buildings and property belonging to the Commonwealth of Kentucky after the exposition closes. It also allows for the return and permanent exhibition of movable exhibits of sufficient interest, with the Governor's approval. The commission is required to settle its accounts and submit a final report to the Governor within six months of the exposition's close, and this report will be submitted to the General Assembly. The act also mandates regular reporting of expenditures and liabilities to the Governor and requires the commission to maintain a record of its proceedings open for inspection.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the disposition of property and exhibits from the Louisiana Purchase Exposition. It outlines the responsibilities of a commission established for this purpose, including selling property, returning exhibits, reporting expenditures, and settling accounts. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to regulate the holding of circuit courts in counties in which there are towns over seventeen miles from the county seat, and having a larger population than the county seat.
classification.summary: This act dictates that in counties with a town over seventeen miles from the county seat and a larger population than the county seat (based on the 1900 federal enumeration), circuit courts will be held alternately between the county seat and the larger town. Civil cases arising in the county seat will be tried there, while civil cases where the first named defendant resides nearest to the larger town will be tried at the larger town. Criminal cases will also be held alternately.

classification.title: Co-operation with director of U. S. geological survey.
classification.summary: This statute authorizes the director of the Kentucky Survey to enter into cooperative topographical surveys with the Director of the United States Geological Survey. It specifies that the U.S. Geological Survey must match the expenditure of the Kentucky Survey for such work. The director is also required to report annually to the Governor on the survey's progress and expenditures. Furthermore, it prohibits employees and officials of the survey from engaging in speculation or private dealings in mineral lands, or from sharing private information about discoveries, with a limited exception for landowners. They are also prohibited from making private reports for personal compensation, unless approved by the director and Governor.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act for government of cities of the first class, approved July 1, 1893, and to repeal section 40 of said act, and to substitute a section for said section 40.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the government of first-class cities in Kentucky. It repeals section 40 of the 1893 act, which established executive boards (Board of Public Works and Board of Public Safety) with members appointed by the Mayor and approved by the Board of Aldermen for a four-year term. The new section substitutes this with a provision that establishes the same executive boards, but members are appointed by the Mayor and are removable at the Mayor's pleasure.

CHAPTER 26 .
AN ACT in regard to the collection of checks and drafts .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That in order to hold the maker , indorser ,
guarantor, or surety of any check or draft deposited
with or forwarded to any individualor bank for col-
lection, or owned by any individualor bank , it shall
be sufficient for said individual or bank to forward the
same in the usual commercial way now in use, accord-
ing to the regular course of business, and the same shall
be considered due diligence in the collection of such
check or draft .
§ 2. All laws which are in conflict with this act
are hereby repealed .
Approved March 11 , 1904 .

classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct acts. The first act, Chapter 29, concerns the regulation of crime and punishment for stealing poultry. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly targets any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The second act, Chapter 30, is an amendment to the Kentucky Constitution regarding election procedures, specifically changing from secret ballots to viva voce (oral) voting. While changes to voting procedures can be relevant to Jim Crow laws, this particular amendment, as presented, does not contain any racial indicators and its primary purpose appears to be a procedural change rather than a disenfranchisement mechanism. Given the lack of racial indicators in either act, and the general nature of the first act, the classification is 'no' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high because the text is clear and lacks any suggestive elements.

classification.title: Amendment to Section 147 of the Kentucky Constitution Regarding Voter Registration and Voting Methods
classification.summary: This statute proposes an amendment to Section 147 of the Kentucky Constitution. The amendment mandates registration for voters in cities and towns with a population of five thousand or more, and allows for general law registration for other voters. It also specifies that where registration is required, only registered individuals can vote. The amendment further clarifies that all elections, including decisions on submitted questions and the choice of officers, shall be viva voce and made a matter of public record. The text indicates this amendment was submitted to voters for ratification.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns voter registration and the method of voting (viva voce). While voter registration laws were often used as a tool to disenfranchise Black voters during the Jim Crow era, this particular amendment, as presented in the text, does not contain any explicit or implicit racial components. It applies to all voters within the specified population thresholds. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high because the text clearly outlines the provisions without any racial language or discriminatory intent evident within the provided excerpt. The category is 'voting' as it directly addresses electoral processes. The document type is 'amendments' as it explicitly states it is an amendment to the Constitution and was submitted to voters for ratification.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend section 1 of an act, entitled "An Act to authorize trustees of county seminary and school property, owned or held in trust by trustees by a majority of their board, to dispose of same to trustees of common school districts embracing same," approved March 17, 1896.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law to allow trustees of county seminaries, established before December 18, 1865, to sell or dispose of seminary property to trustees of white common school districts. The property or its proceeds must be used for the education of white pupils in those districts, with provisions for white pupils from other districts to receive a reduction to compensate for their interest in the seminary property.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that the seminary property can be sold to 'trustees of white common school districts' and that the property or proceeds 'shall be used or disposed of by such trustees for the purpose of the school for white pupils in such districts'. This direct mention of 'white' in relation to the beneficiaries of the educational property clearly indicates a racial segregation provision, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The category is 'education' because the statute deals with the disposition of school property for educational purposes. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language. Human review is not needed as the racial intent is clear.

classification.summary: This act amends existing statutes to require individuals practicing dentistry or dental surgery for compensation to possess a diploma from an authorized dental college and a certificate of qualification issued by the Kentucky State Board of Dental Examiners. It also removes provisions related to the election of examiners by the State Dental Association.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the structure of an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly focused on professional licensing and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 2638 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to the Board of Dental Examiners
classification.summary: This act establishes the Kentucky State Board of Dental Examiners, outlining its composition, appointment process, and term lengths. It also details the board's meeting schedule for examining applicants and transacting business, and the process for electing officers. The act specifies qualifications for board members, requiring at least five years of practice in the state and prohibiting affiliation with dental colleges. It also exempts individuals holding certificates from the Kentucky State Dental Association from further examination.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation and licensing of dentists in Kentucky by establishing a state board of examiners. It details the qualifications, appointment, and operational procedures of this board. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year 1904, which refers to a legislative session.

classification.title: An Act to Regulate the Practice of Dentistry
classification.summary: This statute establishes the Kentucky State Board of Dental Examiners, outlines the requirements for practicing dentistry in the Commonwealth, including a mandatory examination and registration process. It details the fees associated with examinations and re-examinations, and mandates the board to keep a record of registered dentists and issue certificates of registration. It also requires dentists to record their certificates with the county clerk.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a standard professional licensing law. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Chapter 85, Article 3 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to the Practice of Dentistry
classification.summary: This act amends existing statutes concerning the practice of dentistry in Kentucky. It requires individuals to obtain a diploma from an authorized dental college and a certificate of qualification from the Kentucky State Board of Dental Examiners to practice dentistry for compensation. It also continues the Kentucky State Dental Association and establishes the Kentucky State Board of Dental Examiners, appointed by the Governor. Exemptions are provided for students in dental college clinics and for those already holding a certificate from the Kentucky State Dental Association. Matriculates attending dental colleges in Kentucky are also exempt from examination requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the regulation and licensing of the practice of dentistry. It outlines the educational and examination requirements for dentists. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly in 1904, amending existing statutes.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 128 of the Kentucky Statutes, Titled "Militia"
classification.summary: This act amends section 128 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the Militia. It removes a provision that required cities of the first and second classes to provide armories at their own cost. Instead, it mandates that the Fiscal Court of any county where State Guard companies are organized and furnished with public arms or equipment by the Governor shall provide an armory at the cost of the county. It also amends section 18 of the same act, detailing the composition and duties of regimental/battalion and company councils of administration, including the election of honorary members who are exempt from jury service upon payment of an annual fee.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the organization and funding of the State Guard and its administrative councils. It does not contain any provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race, nor does it relate to any of the typical categories associated with Jim Crow laws (education, housing, marriage, public accommodations, incarceration, voting, labor). Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly. The racial indicator is 'none' as race is not mentioned or implied.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and re-enact section 6 of an act, entitled "An Act concerning conveyances," approved April 22, 1893, and being section 495 of the Kentucky Statutes.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous statute concerning conveyances. It requires that all deeds, mortgages, and other instruments of writing intended to be effectual against purchasers without notice or creditors must be recorded in the county clerk's office where the property is located. Crucially, it makes it unlawful for county clerks or their deputies to record any deed of conveyance for real estate interests equal to or greater than a life estate unless the deed clearly specifies and refers to the immediate source from which the grantor derived title. If the source is a recorded deed or writing, the new deed must reference its recording information (office, book, page, date). If the title was obtained by inheritance or other means not through a recorded instrument, the deed must clearly state how and from whom the grantor obtained the title.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the requirements for recording deeds and other instruments of conveyance for real estate. It mandates that the source of title must be clearly stated in the deed for it to be recorded. This is a procedural requirement related to property law and does not contain any explicit or implicit racial components. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text is clear and directly addresses property conveyance procedures without any mention or implication of race. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the description of the act as passed by the 'General Assembly'.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to regulate the business of foreign corporations doing business in this State," approved March 10, 1894, and to provide for the appointment of agents upon whom process may be served.
classification.summary: This act requires non-resident individuals, partnerships, associations, and joint-stock companies engaged in business in Kentucky to file the name and residence of a local agent with the Secretary of State. This agent will be the point of contact for service of process on any bond issued by the entity. Failure to comply results in a misdemeanor charge and fines for the entity and its agents or employees. The act declares an emergency, taking effect immediately upon approval.

The confidence is high because the text clearly outlines business regulations without any racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend section 1054, Kentucky Statutes, which is section 34, article 3 of an act, entitled "An Act relating to courts of justice," which became a law and was approved by the Governor June 10, 1893.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 1054 of the Kentucky Statutes by changing the language regarding appeals from justice, fiscal, city, or police courts. It replaces the condition "When the value in controversy is over ten dollars, exclusive of interest and cost" with "in civil cases." It also includes a proviso that in cases involving a question of exemption, the defendant can appeal without executing a bond.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The changes are procedural and relate to the jurisdiction and process of the courts. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of and to reimburse cemetery companies in this State for the over-payment of taxes.
classification.summary: This act provides reimbursement to cemetery companies that overpaid taxes in 1903. It addresses a situation where companies paid taxes for ten years prior to 1903, when legal precedent (Commonwealth v. Nute) established that they should have only been liable for five years. The act aims to correct this overpayment and ensure fairness by limiting back tax liability to five years, aligning with the Nute decision.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the reimbursement of cemetery companies for overpaid taxes due to a change in legal interpretation regarding the statute of limitations for tax liability. It does not mention race, nor does it contain any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the counties of this Commonwealth to issue bonds for the construction of turnpikes and gravel roads.
classification.summary: This act grants fiscal courts the authority to issue and sell county bonds for the construction of turnpike or gravel roads. The bonds are to be prepared, executed, and issued under the seal of the county clerk, signed by the presiding judge and countersigned by the county court clerk. The bonds will be payable to bearer at a designated place and time, with a maturity of not less than two nor more than thirty years from the date of issuance. The repayment time can be optional for the county within these limits. The bonds will not bear interest exceeding six percent per annum, payable annually or semi-annually, with interest coupons attached.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Authorize Fiscal Courts to Issue Bonds for Railroad Purposes" Approved March 10, 1894
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the issuance of bonds by fiscal courts for railroad purposes. It outlines procedures for issuing new bonds to exchange for existing ones, or for selling new bonds to raise money to pay off existing railroad bonds. Key provisions include public notice requirements (weekly newspaper publication and posted notices) and the right of voters to petition for a referendum on the proposed action. If a petition signed by 300 voters is filed, the fiscal court cannot proceed until a majority of legal voters approve the action in an election.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the financial procedures of fiscal courts regarding railroad bonds. It outlines requirements for public notification and voter approval for issuing or exchanging bonds. classification.title: An Act to Regulate the Practice of Embalming
classification.summary: This act establishes requirements and qualifications for practicing embalming in Kentucky, effective July 1, 1904. It mandates written applications, license fees, and examinations by the State Board of Embalming. Applicants must demonstrate good moral character, skill in embalming and care of the dead, and knowledge of sanitation and disinfection, especially in cases of infectious diseases. Licenses are valid for one year and must be registered with local health boards or county clerks and displayed publicly. Licenses are not transferable and expire annually on July 31st.

It focuses on professional qualifications, moral character, and public health aspects related to the practice of embalming. While election laws were often a target of Jim Crow legislation, this particular amendment, as presented, does not contain any explicit or implicit racial components. It appears to be a procedural change in election administration. Therefore, based on the provided text, this statute is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

It specifies that the sheriff of the county shall be a member of the board and preside at its meetings. It also outlines the appointment of two commissioners, one from each of the two major political parties, based on nominations from county executive committees. The amendment also addresses situations where the sheriff cannot act, allowing the circuit court clerk to serve in their place.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the organization and appointment of county election commissioners. While it mentions the sheriff's role and the involvement of political parties, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race or any discriminatory provisions. The focus is on the administrative and political structure of election boards. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text clearly defines the scope of the law without any racial implications. The category is 'voting' as it directly relates to election administration.

classification.title: AN ACT to secure school attendance of children between the ages of seven and fourteen.
classification.summary: This act requires parents or guardians in cities of the first, second, third, and fourth classes to send children between the ages of seven and fourteen to school for the full term. It also establishes the role and appointment of truant officers to enforce school attendance. Exceptions are made for children who are physically or mentally unfit or have already acquired the required common school branches.

It outlines the responsibilities of parents/guardians and the appointment of truant officers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the statute's text is clear and directly addresses educational requirements without any racial component.

classification.title: An Act to Provide for the Enforcement of the Compulsory Attendance Law
classification.summary: This act outlines the duties of truant officers in enforcing compulsory school attendance for children between the ages of seven and fourteen. It details the process of notifying parents or guardians of truancy, the requirement for children to attend a recognized school within five days of notice, and penalties for non-compliance, including fines for first and subsequent offenses. It also addresses penalties for making false statements about a child's age or school attendance.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses compulsory school attendance and the enforcement mechanisms for truancy. It sets age ranges for mandatory attendance and outlines penalties for parents or guardians who fail to ensure their children attend school. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply universally to all children within the specified age range. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly focused on educational enforcement without any racial component.

classification.summary: This act amends section 3704 of the Kentucky Statutes, specifically subsection 1, paragraph one, concerning the powers of the Board of Trustees for towns of the sixth class. It adds provisions allowing the board to contract for water and lights, purchase, lease, or receive real and personal property for municipal purposes, and control, dispose of, and convey such property. It also includes a proviso that the board cannot sell or convey any portion of a water front but may rent it for up to twenty years, with wharf privileges limited to one year.

CHAPTER 97 .
AN ACT ceding to the United States jurisdiction over a sitefora
camp of instruction and miltary post.
WHEREAS , the State of Kentucky , by reason ofher
central position in the Union , and her superior facil-
itiesfor furnishing good army horses and for trans-
porting troops and munitions of war , quickly and
cheaply to allparts of the Union , is admirably suited
for the location of a permanent camp of instruction
and military post, now , therefore,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
That in the event of the establishment by the gen-
eral Government of a permanent camp of instruction
and militarypost in the vicinityof West Point, Ken-
tucky, commonly known as the West Point Site, the
State of Kentucky hereby relinquishesand cedes to
the United States all the rights and jurisdiction
which she now possesses over the land and premises
conveyed or to be conveyed to the United States for
the purpose of such permanent camp of instruction
and military post, and exempt the said premises and
all Government property thereon from all taxes and
assessments so long as the same shall remain the
property of the United States.
§ 2. There being no law in forcenow authoriz
ing the purposes set out in the firstsectionof this
act, an emergency is thereforedeclaredto exist, and
this act shall take effectand be in full force from and
after its passage .
Approved March 22, 1904 .

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Section 1611 of Chapter 32 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to the Condemnation of Land and Material for Public Roads
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for condemning land and material for public road construction in Kentucky. It details procedures for appeals, bond requirements, and the prosecution of condemnation cases in the name of the Commonwealth for the benefit of the county. It also specifies when the Road Supervisor can take possession of the land after damages and costs are paid or deposited.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal procedures for land condemnation for public road construction. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment for different racial groups. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the approval date of a specific legislative session.

classification.title: ARTICLE II. Consideration
classification.summary: This article outlines the legal presumptions and definitions related to consideration in negotiable instruments. It states that negotiable instruments are presumed to be issued for valuable consideration, and parties are presumed to have become parties for value. It defines value as any consideration sufficient for a simple contract, including antecedent or pre-existing debts. It also clarifies that a holder is considered a holder for value if value has been given at any time, and a holder with a lien is a holder for value to the extent of the lien. Absence or failure of consideration is a defense against non-holders in due course, and partial failure is a defense pro tanto. An accommodation party is defined as someone who signs without receiving value to lend their name to another, and they are liable to a holder for value even if the holder knew they were an accommodation party.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section amends Section 190 of Article 5 of an act, which is Section 771 of the Kentucky Statutes. The amendment grants corporations the power to borrow money for various purposes, including funding floating debt, completing, equipping, or operating their road, or paying debts incurred for such purposes. It also allows them to issue bonds, mortgage their property and franchises, and confer rights to convert bonds into stock or preferred stock into bonds. The stock can be sold at various times and places, with proceeds applied for the purpose of issuance. Additionally, it outlines a process for corporations with only common stock to take certain actions through a resolution adopted by a vote of stockholders.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 567 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Corporations
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 567 to allow corporations to distribute or convert their outstanding capital stock into preferred and common stock. It outlines the procedures for such a conversion, including notice requirements and stockholder consent. It also details the rights of preferred stockholders regarding dividends and redemption upon dissolution. The act specifies that preferred stock dividends are payable before common stock dividends, and preferred stockholders have priority in asset distribution upon dissolution.

classification.title: Condemnation of property for park purposes
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 89 of an act, which is Section 2852 of the Kentucky Statutes. It grants the Board of Park Commissioners the power to condemn property for park purposes, either within or beyond the city limits, located in the same county. The condemnation proceedings are to be conducted in the Jefferson Circuit Court by the city attorney, following ordinary legal procedures. It also specifies requirements for warning orders against non-residents, absent defendants, or unknown owners, including publication in two daily papers.

It outlines the legal process for acquiring land for parks, including the authority of the Board of Park Commissioners and the procedures in court. classification.summary: This statute outlines regulations for cities of the second class in Kentucky that are in arrears on their debts. It restricts changes to city limits, caps the aggregate salaries of city officers to ten percent of the previous year's treasury intake (with exceptions for current officeholders), limits the annual ad valorem tax rate to fifty cents per hundred dollars of taxable property, and prohibits issuing warrants without sufficient funds. The act is from the 1904 session laws.

classification.title: An Act relating to the bonds of city treasurers in cities of the Fourth Class.
classification.summary: This act outlines requirements for city treasurers in cities of the Fourth Class regarding their bonds. It specifies that failure to provide a bond within thirty days of election or appointment will result in forfeiture of office and ineligibility for re-election or re-appointment for two years. The act also states that the city will pay the premiums for these bonds from its general fund. It clarifies its relationship with existing statutes and its duration of applicability, which is tied to the city's payment of its matured obligations.

The confidence is high because the content is clearly administrative and financial, with no discernible racial component.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the assessment of property for taxation, and to levy a tax thereon," approved March 15, 1898.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the assessment of property for taxation. It specifies that the county clerk shall furnish the assessor with a list of all persons and corporations owning or controlling property subject to taxation, and that the assessor shall assess all such property at its fair cash value. The act also details the process for appealing assessments and the penalties for failing to list property.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is an amendment to a taxation law. It details procedures for property assessment and taxation.
